barnaby-joyce-new-england.summary: Held New Zealand citizenship by descent from his father, which the High Court found rendered him ineligible under section 44(i); his 2016 election was declared invalid on 27 October 2017. Having renounced his New Zealand citizenship in August 2017, he was eligible to recontest and won the December 2017 New England by-election.

anthony-albanese-grayndler.summary: Although birth to an Italian father would ordinarily confer Italian citizenship, his father was not recorded on his birth certificate, so he was not a dual citizen and met the eligibility requirements of section 44 of the Constitution.

alex-hawke-mitchell.summary: During the 2017 citizenship questions over members with foreign-born parents, Hawke stated he is 'an Australian citizen only and have never held or acquired or sought Greek or any other citizenship.' He was not referred to the High Court.

sources.title: I'm not a Greek citizen: Liberal MP Alex Hawke

dai-le-fowler.summary: After her 2022 election, questions were raised about her section 44 eligibility given her refugee background; she stated on her AEC declaration that she had never been a subject or citizen of any country other than Australia and confirmed the AEC accepted her nomination, rejecting suggestions of ineligibility.

sources.title: Dai Le rejects suggestions she's ineligible for Australia's parliament due to section 44

emma-mcbride-dobell.summary: Obtained Irish citizenship by descent (through a grandfather born in Northern Ireland) in 2001 but renounced it before standing for parliament at the 2013 federal election, resolving her foreign citizenship for section 44 eligibility purposes.

jerome-laxale-bennelong.summary: Inherited French and Mauritian citizenship from his parents and renounced both (recorded as held until 2022) before standing at the 2022 federal election, in order to comply with section 44 of the Constitution.

sussan-ley-farrer.summary: Ley was born overseas (in Kano, Nigeria, to English parents) and has publicly stated that she renounced her other citizenship before sitting in the Australian Parliament, taking a few months to do so in order to satisfy section 44(i) eligibility.

llew-o-brien-wide-bay.summary: After the Parliament of Canada passed Bill C-3 (in force 15 December 2025), O'Brien acquired Canadian citizenship by descent through his Canadian-born father and grandfather; on 6 February 2026 he announced his intention to renounce that Canadian citizenship because section 44(i) of the Constitution prohibits parliamentarians from holding dual citizenship.

rebekha-sharkie-mayo.summary: Sharkie was born in England and held British citizenship by birth; she had not completed renunciation before nominating for the 2016 election, so following the High Court's Gallagher ruling she resigned in May 2018 and recontested. She was re-elected at the Mayo by-election on 28 July 2018 after her British citizenship was renounced, resolving her section 44(i) eligibility.

tony-zappia-makin.summary: Zappia was born in Italy in 1952 and naturalised as an Australian citizen in 1958. He stated he ceased to be an Italian citizen automatically upon acquiring Australian citizenship in 1958 (Italy then stripping citizenship from those naturalising elsewhere), a position he said the Italian Consul confirmed by letter dated July 2004, supporting his section 44(i) eligibility.

sources.title: Labor MP denies Italian dual citizenship as Coalition threatens counter-action in court

andrew-giles-scullin.summary: Giles was a British citizen by descent through his UK-born mother. He renounced his British citizenship before standing for parliament in 2013, resolving his section 44(i) eligibility.

daniel-mulino-fraser.summary: Mulino was born in Brindisi, Italy, and stated in his 2019 maiden speech that he had renounced his (Italian) citizenship "long ago," addressing his eligibility to sit under section 44(i).

anne-aly-cowan.summary: Born in Egypt, Aly renounced her Egyptian citizenship on 6 May 2016, before the close of nominations for the 2016 federal election, so she did not breach section 44(i); the Egyptian embassy later provided written confirmation that as of that date she had completely renounced her Egyptian citizenship.

sources.title: Dual citizenship: Labor releases letter from Egyptian embassy clearing Anne Aly

josh-wilson-fremantle.summary: Born in London, Wilson held British citizenship and applied to renounce it shortly after his 2016 endorsement, with the renunciation registered effective 29 June 2016 (after the close of nominations). Referred to the High Court, he resigned in May 2018 following the Gallagher ruling and won the resulting Fremantle by-election.

katy-gallagher-act.summary: Gallagher held British citizenship by descent through her father; her renunciation was lodged in April 2016 but not registered by the UK Home Office until 16 August 2016, after she nominated for the 2016 election. On 9 May 2018 the High Court unanimously ruled her ineligible under section 44(i); she returned to the Senate at the 2019 election.

david-pocock-act.summary: Pocock, who held Zimbabwean and South African citizenship by birth/descent, provided documentary evidence renouncing both before the nomination deadline of 22 April 2022, leaving him solely an Australian citizen as required by section 44 ahead of his 2022 Senate election.

sources.title: David Pocock to run for ACT Senate with Clare Doube, renounces Zimbabwean South African citizenships

ben-small-forrest.summary: Small discovered he held Australian-New Zealand dual citizenship and resigned from the Senate on 15 April 2022 under section 44(i); New Zealand approved his renunciation on 14 April 2022, after which he was reappointed to fill the vacancy, and he was later elected MP for Forrest in 2025.

sam-lim-tangney.summary: Born in Malaysia, Lim became an Australian citizen in 2008; his Wikipedia citizenship record lists his Malaysian citizenship as held until 2021, before his 2022 election to the House of Representatives.

andrew-bragg-nsw.summary: Bragg was a British citizen by descent (his father and three grandparents were born in the United Kingdom) until renouncing it in December 2017, before his 2019 election to the Senate.

deborah-o-neill-nsw.summary: O'Neill, who held Irish citizenship through her Irish parents, renounced her Irish citizenship in order to run for federal parliament as required by section 44.

sources.title: Third Australian politician admits to holding dual nationality

matthew-canavan-qld.summary: Referred to the High Court in the 2017-18 citizenship crisis over possible Italian citizenship by descent; on 27 October 2017 the Court (Re Canavan) held he had never been an Italian citizen and was validly elected, so he was not disqualified under section 44(i).

malcolm-roberts-qld.summary: Born in India in 1955 and a British citizen by descent through his father; his attempts to renounce before nominating were ineffectual and renunciation was only registered on 5 December 2016. The High Court ruled on 27 October 2017 that he had been ineligible under section 44(i) and disqualified him; with his citizenship resolved he was re-elected to the Senate in 2019.

larissa-waters-qld.summary: Held Canadian citizenship from birth; she resigned from the Senate on 18 July 2017 over section 44(i), renounced her Canadian citizenship on 8 August 2017, and the High Court ruled on 27 October 2017 that she had been invalidly elected. She returned to the Senate on 6 September 2018 to fill a casual vacancy.

mehreen-faruqi-nsw.summary: Migrated from Pakistan in 1992 and became an Australian citizen in 1994; she retained Pakistani citizenship until renouncing it in 2017 to meet section 44 eligibility requirements before entering federal Parliament.

tim-ayres-nsw.summary: A change to Canadian citizenship law (Bill C-3, passed 15 December 2025) automatically conferred Canadian citizenship on him via a Canadian-born grandfather while he was already a sitting senator; he renounced the Canadian citizenship on 29 December 2025, which did not affect his eligibility under section 44(i).

penny-wong-sa.summary: Wong was born in Kota Kinabalu, Malaysia, and held Malaysian citizenship, which she renounced in 2001 before being elected to the Senate in 2002, resolving her eligibility under section 44(i).

marielle-smith-sa.summary: Smith held British citizenship by descent through her UK-born mother and renounced it in 2018 prior to nominating for parliament, resolving her eligibility under section 44(i).

jacqui-lambie-tas.summary: Lambie held British citizenship by descent from her Scottish-born father and resigned from the Senate on 14 November 2017 under section 44 over this dual citizenship; following a recount her seat went to Steve Martin, and she was re-elected to the Senate in 2019.

nick-mckim-tas.summary: McKim was born in London and held British citizenship, which he formally renounced (registered 1 February 2016); he sat in breach of section 44 from 20 August 2015 until that date, but his renunciation was complete before the 2016 election nominations, so he was not in breach when nominating.

michelle-ananda-rajah-vic.summary: Born in London, Michelle Ananda-Rajah became an Australian citizen in 1996 and renounced her British citizenship in 2021, resolving her foreign-citizenship status for the purposes of section 44(i) eligibility.

ralph-babet-vic.summary: Born on Rodrigues in the Republic of Mauritius and an Australian citizen since 1993, Ralph Babet renounced his Mauritian citizenship in March 2022, before taking his Senate seat, to satisfy section 44(i) eligibility.

fatima-payman-wa.summary: Born in Kabul, Afghanistan, and naturalised as an Australian citizen in 2005, Fatima Payman was advised she had taken "all reasonable steps" (per Sykes v Cleary) to renounce her Afghan citizenship after a 2021 renunciation request could not be finalised; a November 2024 Senate motion to investigate her section 44 eligibility was defeated 35-3.

jordon-steele-john-wa.summary: Born in Northampton, England, Jordon Steele-John held British citizenship from 1994 to 2013 and renounced it at age eighteen in preparation for the 2013 federal election, leaving him solely an Australian citizen for section 44(i) purposes.
